A soggy couple of days in Utah with snow down to some valley locations. The "dreaded lake effect" brought a band of heavy snow to the western half of the Salt Lake Valley late on the 6th and early on the seventh, with Herriman picking up 9 inches of snow in just 2 hours. Other snow totals included 26 inches in Alta, 21 inches at Brian Head, 9 inches in East Layton, 8 inches in Ibapah, 6 inches in Tooele, 5 inches in Cedar City and 4 inches in Pleasant Grove.

CZ_NAME: W UTAH T X NW/P X E JUAB
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm really pounded the mountains with heavy snow. Some of the totals reported over the tree day period included 55 inches at Farnsworth Lake, 48 inches at the Gooseberry Ranger Station, 31 inches at Solitude, 28 inches at Brian Head and 27 inches at Brighton. The Tooele bench reported 10 inches, Cedar City 9 inches, and Nephi had 5 inches.

A powerful winter storm roared across pretty much the whole state. Even Utah's Dixie received measurable snow. Slick roads contributed to hundreds of minor traffic accidents. Though the snow amounts were not overly impressive, it was the widespread nature of the event that made it unusual. The highest total reported was 14 inches at Brian Head. Most mountain locations picked up 6-10 inches, with some valley totals including 7 inches in South Ogden, 5 inches in Cedar City, 4 inches at Delta, 3 inches in Zion National Park and 1 inch in St. George.

CZ_NAME: W SUMMIT T NW/SE SALT LAKE/WASATCH X SC/E UTAH/P X E JUAB/X NW SAN PETE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Wasatch Mountains and valleys, with 7 inches reported at Kimball Junction, 6 inches in Coalville and Alta, and 5 inches at Snowbird.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flash flooding occurred at Capitol Reef National Park, with water up to Silver Creek bridge. Highway 24 was reported under water with boulders rolling across the highway. Park visitors were being evacuated, and the Fremont river was 5 feet over flood stage.

CZ_NAME: W SALT LAKE T X W/E TOOLE T X E
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring storm that brought the strong winds to the state brought a good dose of snow after the winds shifted and died down. Most of the snow was confined to the northern mountains and Wasatch Front. Some of the higher totals included 21 inches at Alta, 19 inches at Snowbird, 14 inches at Brighton and 11 inches at Solitude. In the lower elevations, Liberty reported 9 inches, Pineview Dam 8 inches, Upper Millcreek 7 inches, Brigham City and Alpine 6 inches, Centerville and South Ogden 5 inches and Provo 4 inches.

A strong cold front accompanied by thunder-snow ushered winter back into Utah. Over 2 feet of powder fell in the mountains, with up to a foot in some valley locations. Some of the highest amounts were 33 inches at Snowbird, 28 inches at Alta, 21 inches at Farnsworth Lake, 20 inches at Brian Head, 14 inches in Kimball Junction, 12 inches in Fillmore, 10 inches in Tooele and Alpine, 9 inches in Laketown and 8 inches in North Salt Lake.

CZ_NAME: RICH/CACHE T NW/P E C WEBER/MOST OF MORGAN/NW SUMMIT/NE SALT LAKE/X NE BOX ELDER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mammoth 3-day winter storm blasted portions of Utah, just in time for Christmas travelers. The worst hit area was from the Tooele Valley east through Salt Lake and Davis Counties, and east into the Wasatch Mountains. Freeways and side streets alike were snow packed and slick, resulting in nearly 1500 accidents statewide. Amazingly, no fatalities were reported from the accidents and injuries were minor. The heavy wet snow knocked out power to at least 70,000 people, mainly in Salt Lake and Davis Counties, as tree limbs fell on power lines. Almost 10,000 people went without pour for 48 hours or more, causing emergency shelters to be opened in Salt Lake City and Ogden. The threat of avalanches closed Little Cottonwood Canyon and placed Snowbird and Alta on inter-lodge restrictions. An empty light rail train derailed near the University of Utah, with dozens of airline flights being delayed or cancelled at the Salt Lake City International Airport, though one runway was kept open throughout the storm. The 10.6 inches of snow that fell at the Salt Lake Airport on the 26th smashed the record snowfall for the date of 4.3 inches set in 1936, and was the second snowiest December day on record. Some of the impressive snow totals included, Snowbird 65 inches, Alta 64 inches, Farmington Canyon 63 inches, Spruces 53 inches, Solitude 47 inches, Snowbasin 45 inches, The Canyons 40 inches and Brian Head 30 inches. And in the lower elevations, Upper Millcreek had an amazing 40 inches, East Layton 32 inches, Bountiful 30 inches, Centerville 28 inches, Kimball Junction 26 inches, South Ogden 24 inches, Tooele 23 inches, Salt Lake City Airport 19 inches, Spanish Fork 15 inches, Logan 14 inches, Brigham City 13 inches and Alpine, Fillmore and Morgan with 10 inches. It was a skiers dream, but a travelers nightmare.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Johnson Canyon just east of Kanab, washing out a bridge and creating some road damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another potent winter storm hit much of Utah, with the northern part of the state the hardest hit. Weather was believed to be the main factor in 2 traffic fatalities. Several hundred minor accidents and fender benders occurred up and down the Wasatch Front, with no major injuries reported. Strong south winds ahead of the cold front caused blowing and drifting snow, forcing Hill Air Force Base and Weber State University to close for awhile. The winds and snow also closed Interstate 15 north of Ogden for several hours due to multiple accidents. Farmington Canyon reported a 3-day snowfall of 40 inches, with most Northern Mountain locations in the 20-30 inch range. The Wasatch Front valleys and benches along with the mountain valleys were generally in the 6-12 inch range, but East Layton came in with 19 inches of fresh snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding in St. George caused water to flow across the Ft. Pierce bridge.
Flash flooding and rock slides were reported over Highway 143 southeast of Parowan.
Heavy snow fell in portions of Duchesne County, with 6 inches being reported in Myton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm really pounded the mountains with heavy snow. Some of the totals reported over the tree day period included 55 inches at Farnsworth Lake, 48 inches at the Gooseberry Ranger Station, 31 inches at Solitude, 28 inches at Brian Head and 27 inches at Brighton. The Tooele bench reported 10 inches, Cedar City 9 inches, and Nephi had 5 inches.

A strong spring storm caused the winds to howl across much of Utah ahead of a cold front. Large sandstorms from recently plowed fields swept across many of the western Utah valleys. Very low visibility in the Eagle Mountain area contributed to a school bus crash. The bus was already going slow when it crashed, so there were no injuries. The wind also flipped a semi trailer on I-80. A tree fell on an apartment building near the University of Utah causing structural damage, and a billboard toppled onto a building in Murray. The winds downed powers lines and blew shingles off roofs in many areas of the Salt Lake Valley. Wind gusts were in the 50 to 70 mph range in the valleys, with 70 to 100 mph wind gusts in some mountain locations.

Strong south winds ahead of the cold front caused blowing and drifting snow, forcing Hill Air Force Base and Weber State University to close for awhile. The winds and snow also closed Interstate 15 north of Ogden for several hours due to multiple accidents. Farmington Canyon reported a 3-day snowfall of 40 inches, with most Northern Mountain locations in the 20-30 inch range. The Wasatch Front valleys and benches along with the mountain valleys were generally in the 6-12 inch range, but East Layton came in with 19 inches of fresh snow.

CZ_NAME: W SALT LAKE T X W/E TOOLE T X E
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mammoth 3-day winter storm blasted portions of Utah, just in time for Christmas travelers. The worst hit area was from the Tooele Valley east through Salt Lake and Davis Counties, and east into the Wasatch Mountains. Freeways and side streets alike were snow packed and slick, resulting in nearly 1500 accidents statewide. Amazingly, no fatalities were reported from the accidents and injuries were minor. The heavy wet snow knocked out power to at least 70,000 people, mainly in Salt Lake and Davis Counties, as tree limbs fell on power lines. Almost 10,000 people went without pour for 48 hours or more, causing emergency shelters to be opened in Salt Lake City and Ogden. The threat of avalanches closed Little Cottonwood Canyon and placed Snowbird and Alta on inter-lodge restrictions. An empty light rail train derailed near the University of Utah, with dozens of airline flights being delayed or cancelled at the Salt Lake City International Airport, though one runway was kept open throughout the storm. The 10.6 inches of snow that fell at the Salt Lake Airport on the 26th smashed the record snowfall for the date of 4.3 inches set in 1936, and was the second snowiest December day on record. Some of the impressive snow totals included, Snowbird 65 inches, Alta 64 inches, Farmington Canyon 63 inches, Spruces 53 inches, Solitude 47 inches, Snowbasin 45 inches, The Canyons 40 inches and Brian Head 30 inches. And in the lower elevations, Upper Millcreek had an amazing 40 inches, East Layton 32 inches, Bountiful 30 inches, Centerville 28 inches, Kimball Junction 26 inches, South Ogden 24 inches, Tooele 23 inches, Salt Lake City Airport 19 inches, Spanish Fork 15 inches, Logan 14 inches, Brigham City 13 inches and Alpine, Fillmore and Morgan with 10 inches. It was a skiers dream, but a travelers nightmare.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was major flash flooding in the town of Parowan. Despite sandbagging efforts, 26 homes were flooded with 6 homes sustaining an estimated $25,000 dollars in damage. People were also stranded for a time in Yankee Meadow, and State Route 143 was closed due to mud and rock slides.

The worst flash flooding in the town of Tropic in 40 years occurred as over 2 inches of rain fell over the area in 90 minutes, sending torrents of mud and water down the center of town and into houses and basements. The floods also tore large propane tanks loose and swept them away. About 30 structures sustained damage, most of them residential homes. No deaths or major injuries were reported.
A 26-year-old hiker was seriously injured after being struck by lightning high on Mt. Timpanogos. He was struck in the head, and the bolt exited his left ankle.

A powerful winter storm roared across pretty much the whole state. Even Utah's Dixie received measurable snow. Slick roads contributed to hundreds of minor traffic accidents. Though the snow amounts were not overly impressive, it was the widespread nature of the event that made it unusual. The highest total reported was 14 inches at Brian Head. Most mountain locations picked up 6-10 inches, with some valley totals including 7 inches in South Ogden, 5 inches in Cedar City, 4 inches at Delta, 3 inches in Zion National Park and 1 inch in St. George.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three snowboarders were killed as a large avalanche swept down the mountain near the Aspen Grove area around Sundance. There were a total of 14 people in the path of the avalanche, but the rest of the skiers and snowboarders were only partially buried and managed to dig themselves out unscathed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm and cold front moved across eastern Utah and produced heavy snow and strong winds across the mountains. Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 feet with local amounts up to 3 and 1/2 feet in the eastern Uinta Mountains. Persistent wind gusts from 25 to 45 mph caused significant blowing and drifting of snow and reduced visibility. A weather spotter 20 miles west of Manila reported drifting up to 4 feet deep with whiteout conditions at times.

A number of winter storms impacted extreme eastern Utah resulting in near normal snowpack by the end of December. One month of near normal snowpack by itself will have little effect on moisture deficits that have accumulated over such a protracted period of time. Therefore, severe to extreme drought conditions persisted across extreme eastern Utah. See the January, 2004 Storm Data publication for a continuation on this drought situation.

Heavy rainfall resulted in widespread flooding and flash flooding in the area.  A law enforcement vehicle en route to rescue stranded residents was caught and washed downstream in a flash flood where the water flowed 5 feet deep.  Both vehicle occupants successfully escaped the flood waters, but were stranded for much of the night.  12 houses in Bluff and 6 houses in the community of Montezuma Creek were flooded with water 2 to 3 feet deep.    Flash flooding was also reported in the community within the White Mesa Indian Reservation.  A sand and gravel company near Bluff was completely inundated with water 20 to 25 feet deep.  All of the vehicles and heavy equipment at the company were destroyed, as well as a large rock crusher.  Many paved county roads were washed out, including large culverts that were obliterated.  Sections of roads up to 60 feet in length were completely destroyed. Roads that were not destroyed were completely covered with large amounts of mud and debris.  The St. Christopher Mission just east of Bluff was flooded.  Several propane tanks were broken off their moorings and washed away.  The San Juan River near Bluff ran at 20,000 cfs during the flooding event and completely covered a frequently used foot bridge for over 12 hours.

CZ_NAME: W SUMMIT T NW/SE SALT LAKE/WASATCH X SC/E UTAH/P X E JUAB/X NW SAN PETE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another blast of snow plastered the Wasatch Front and Northern Utah mountains just in time to wreak havoc on holiday travelers. Nearly 100 traffic accidents were reported from Ogden to Provo during the morning commute. Most were fender benders, and injuries were minor. The "dreaded lake effect" kicked into high gear late on the 26th, adding to the driving woes in Davis and Salt Lake counties, but adding welcome powder the the ski resorts just before Thanksgiving. Some of the higher snow amounts were Alta with 3 feet, Snowbird 28 inches, Brighton 20 inches, Tony Grove Lake 16 inches, Snowbasin 10 inches and Park City 8 inches. Most valley and bench locations picked up 2-8 inches of fresh snow.

A winter storm brought heavy snow and slick roads to much of Northern Utah, causing several accidents. The snow was also blamed for a fatality, as a driver lost control of his vehicle on the snowy roads between Grantsville and Wendover, rolling it into the medium. Some snow totals for this storm were 16 inches at Snowbird and Solitude, 14 inches at Alta, 12 inches at Sundance, 8 inches in Centerville, Bountiful and East Layton, 6 inches in South Ogden and the SLC Avenues, and 4 inches in Clearfield, Alpine, Heber City, Morgan and Laketown.

Another potent winter storm hit much of Utah, with the northern part of the state the hardest hit. Weather was believed to be the main factor in 2 traffic fatalities. Several hundred minor accidents and fender benders occurred up and down the Wasatch Front, with no major injuries reported. Strong south winds ahead of the cold front caused blowing and drifting snow, forcing Hill Air Force Base and Weber State University to close for awhile. The winds and snow also closed Interstate 15 north of Ogden for several hours due to multiple accidents. Farmington Canyon reported a 3-day snowfall of 40 inches, with most Northern Mountain locations in the 20-30 inch range. The Wasatch Front valleys and benches along with the mountain valleys were generally in the 6-12 inch range, but East Layton came in with 19 inches of fresh snow.

A mammoth 3-day winter storm blasted portions of Utah, just in time for Christmas travelers. The worst hit area was from the Tooele Valley east through Salt Lake and Davis Counties, and east into the Wasatch Mountains. Freeways and side streets alike were snow packed and slick, resulting in nearly 1500 accidents statewide. Amazingly, no fatalities were reported from the accidents and injuries were minor. The heavy wet snow knocked out power to at least 70,000 people, mainly in Salt Lake and Davis Counties, as tree limbs fell on power lines. Almost 10,000 people went without pour for 48 hours or more, causing emergency shelters to be opened in Salt Lake City and Ogden. The threat of avalanches closed Little Cottonwood Canyon and placed Snowbird and Alta on inter-lodge restrictions. An empty light rail train derailed near the University of Utah, with dozens of airline flights being delayed or cancelled at the Salt Lake City International Airport, though one runway was kept open throughout the storm. The 10.6 inches of snow that fell at the Salt Lake Airport on the 26th smashed the record snowfall for the date of 4.3 inches set in 1936, and was the second snowiest December day on record. Some of the impressive snow totals included, Snowbird 65 inches, Alta 64 inches, Farmington Canyon 63 inches, Spruces 53 inches, Solitude 47 inches, Snowbasin 45 inches, The Canyons 40 inches and Brian Head 30 inches. And in the lower elevations, Upper Millcreek had an amazing 40 inches, East Layton 32 inches, Bountiful 30 inches, Centerville 28 inches, Kimball Junction 26 inches, South Ogden 24 inches, Tooele 23 inches, Salt Lake City Airport 19 inches, Spanish Fork 15 inches, Logan 14 inches, Brigham City 13 inches and Alpine, Fillmore and Morgan with 10 inches. It was a skiers dream, but a travelers nightmare.

In the valleys and benches, Upper Millcreek, Sandy and Alpine picked up 7 inches, Midvale 6 inches, with Pleasant Grove and the Salt Lake International Airport getting 3 inches.

Other snow totals included 26 inches in Alta, 21 inches at Brian Head, 9 inches in East Layton, 8 inches in Ibapah, 6 inches in Tooele, 5 inches in Cedar City and 4 inches in Pleasant Grove.

A strong spring storm caused the winds to howl across much of Utah ahead of a cold front. Large sandstorms from recently plowed fields swept across many of the western Utah valleys. Very low visibility in the Eagle Mountain area contributed to a school bus crash. The bus was already going slow when it crashed, so there were no injuries. The wind also flipped a semi trailer on I-80. A tree fell on an apartment building near the University of Utah causing structural damage, and a billboard toppled onto a building in Murray. The winds downed powers lines and blew shingles off roofs in many areas of the Salt Lake Valley. Wind gusts were in the 50 to 70 mph range in the valleys, with 70 to 100 mph wind gusts in some mountain locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold storm plunged down from the Gulf of Alaska into Northern Utah. The storm produced decent amounts of snow, but as the temperatures plummeted the roads became like ice rinks. At least 500 accidents occurred up and down the Wasatch Front as cars slid off the roads or into each other. However, only very minor injuries were reported, as most accidents were at a very slow rate of speed or fender benders. Snowfall was generally 2-4 inches in the valleys with 6-12 inches in the mountains.

CZ_NAME: C E SUMMIT/X NE WASATCH/N DUCHESNE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nice little storm brought good amounts of snow to most mountains across the state, with several inches across the northern valleys. Some mountain snow totals included 23 inches at Alta, 18 inches at Snowbird and Brighton, 15 inches at Brian Head and 13 inches at Silver Lake. In the lower elevations of the north, Herriman, Olympus Cove, Upper Millcreek and Duchesne reported 5 inches, with East Layton, Morgan and Coalville picking up 4 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: X NW KANE/XS XE IRON/N W WASHINGTON/NW WC GARFIELD/PIUTE/W WAYNE/SEVIER T C/X E MILLARD/S SANPETE T SC/X SE JUAB/P X NW EMERY/X E BEAVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm really pounded the mountains with heavy snow. Some of the totals reported over the tree day period included 55 inches at Farnsworth Lake, 48 inches at the Gooseberry Ranger Station, 31 inches at Solitude, 28 inches at Brian Head and 27 inches at Brighton. The Tooele bench reported 10 inches, Cedar City 9 inches, and Nephi had 5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms along a cold front blew through Utah County, tearing down tree limbs, blowing shingles off roofs and flipping over a trampoline. Springville reported a gust to 69 mph (60 kts) and Provo BYU recorded a gust to 62 mph (54 kts).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm roared across pretty much the whole state. Even Utah's Dixie received measurable snow. Slick roads contributed to hundreds of minor traffic accidents. Though the snow amounts were not overly impressive, it was the widespread nature of the event that made it unusual. The highest total reported was 14 inches at Brian Head. Most mountain locations picked up 6-10 inches, with some valley totals including 7 inches in South Ogden, 5 inches in Cedar City, 4 inches at Delta, 3 inches in Zion National Park and 1 inch in St. George.

A storm system brought strong winds and heavy snow to the mountains of Northern Utah. A little bit of wet snow fell along the higher benches of the Wasatch Front. Solitude led the pack with 26 inches of fresh powder, followed by Alta with 20 inches, Snowbird 19 inches, Snowbasin 16 inches and Powder Mountain 14 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst wind gust to 58 mph (50 kts) was measured at Signal Peak near Richfield as a cold front moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowstorm that began on Halloween continued to pound portions of Northern Utah into the first day of November. Sporadic power outages were reported along the Wasatch Front. Some of the heavier snow totals included 27 inches at Solitude, 24 inches at Brighton, 22 inches at Alta, 21 inches at Snowbird, 13 inches in Heber City, 12 inches in Mountain Green and Woodland, 11 inches in Randolph, 9 inches in Coalville, and 2-8 inches over the remainder of the Wasatch Front valleys and benches.

An unseasonably cold storm system dumped a thick blanket of snow across the higher elevations of Northern Utah, catching some campers in the High Uintas by surprise. Some of the snow amounts included 6-10 inches in the High Uintas above 7000 feet, 6 inches at Alta and Brighton, and 3 inches at Solitude.

A powerful spring storm moved across the Great Basin and produced strong winds across extreme eastern Utah.  Sustained wind speeds in the lower elevations ranged from 30 to 35 mph, with frequent gusts of 45 to 55 mph.  Winds frequently gusted from 55 to 70 mph across the central and northern mountains.  Visibilities were significantly reduced in some areas due to blowing dust. On Interstate 70 just west of the Colorado border, at about 1400, three semi tractor-trailer rigs were involved in chain reaction collisions when visibilities were reduced to near zero.

A storm system moved across eastern Utah and produced strong winds and snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches across the east central and northeastern mountains.  Wind speeds across the Tavaputs Plateau were estimated between 30 and 40 mph with frequent gusts to 50 mph creating blowing and drifting snow.

Five passenger cars and three tractor trailers were involved in one accident, and a single tractor trailer was involved in another accident. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small scale flash flood was reported by a park employee at Zion National Park.
Flash flooding occurred on the North Fork of the Virgin River between Zion National Park and Springdale. River rises of 110 cubic feet per second were recorded.

CZ_NAME: P NC WEBER/P C MORGAN/P W C SUMMIT/P SC WASATCH
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another blast of snow plastered the Wasatch Front and Northern Utah mountains just in time to wreak havoc on holiday travelers. Nearly 100 traffic accidents were reported from Ogden to Provo during the morning commute. Most were fender benders, and injuries were minor. The "dreaded lake effect" kicked into high gear late on the 26th, adding to the driving woes in Davis and Salt Lake counties, but adding welcome powder the the ski resorts just before Thanksgiving. Some of the higher snow amounts were Alta with 3 feet, Snowbird 28 inches, Brighton 20 inches, Tony Grove Lake 16 inches, Snowbasin 10 inches and Park City 8 inches. Most valley and bench locations picked up 2-8 inches of fresh snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The town of Modena reported heavy rain with small hail and localized street flooding. The storm dumped 1.30 inches of rain in less than 30 minutes, and the wind and hail broke some windows.
Strong microburst winds gusting to 63 mph (55 kts) move through the Wendover area.
Flash flooding was reported in the Narrows headwater drainage. Gauges detected a rise of 160 cubic feet per second at Springdale.
Thunderstorms dumped 3/4 inch hail in and around North Ogden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the Gulch area east of Capitol Reef National Park, and in the Paria River headwater area.
Flash flooding was reported in the Gulch area east of Capitol Reef National Park, and in the Paria River headwater area.

This wildfire became known as the Hammond Fire. The fire occurred in the Manti-LaSal National Forest and consumed 171 acres of mountain brush, juniper, and pinyon pine. The estimated cost to fight the fire was 60 thousand dollars.

Lightning struck a government employee duplex at the Needles housing area of Canyonlands National Park.  A hole was blown in the roof of the duplex.  The phone system in the housing community was knocked out of service.  The thunderstorm which produced this damaging lightning bolt also ignited two wildfires near the housing complex.

This human caused fire began on June 28. It occurred in the Dark Canyon Wilderness area of the Manti-LaSal National Forest. The fire consumed 2,710 acres of mixed conifer forest and brush. The estimated cost of fighting the fire was 568 thousand dollars.
This wildfire became known as the Diamond Breaks Fire. The fire consumed 160 acres of pinyon pine and juniper on Bureau of Land Management public lands.
This wildfire became known as the Cottonwood Fire. The fire occurred on the Tavaputs Plateau and consumed 300 acres of fir and oak brush.
